entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: PROPHECY THE HIGHEST GIFT.
entries.body: Paul says that prophecy is the highest gift after love because it is to the benefit and advantage of the church, since by it everybody learns the principles of God’s law. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: WHY THE CORINTHIANS VALUED TONGUES.
entries.body: The Corinthians thought that speaking in tongues was a great gift because it was the one which the apostles received first, and with a great display. But this was no reason to think it was the greatest gift of all. The reason the apostles got it first was because it was a sign that they were to go everywhere, preaching the gospel. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 35.1

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: UPBUILDING, ENCOURAGEMENT AND CONSOLATION.
entries.body: A person is built up when he finds out the answer to disputed points. Encouragement comes to him when he is enabled to endure. He is consoled when he continues in hope even when others are viewing his discipline with contempt. Knowledge of the law strengthens his soul and encourages him to hope for better things. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: TO THE BENEFIT OF EVERYONE.
entries.body: Paul reckons this gift as a higher one because it is used for the common good. He always gives the greater honor to those gifts that will be used for the benefit of everyone. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 35.1

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE PROPHETS INTERPRET SCRIPTURE.
entries.body: Paul is saying that prophets are interpreters of the Scriptures. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: INDIVIDUAL AND CHURCH EDIFICATION.
entries.body: The difference between tongues and prophecy is precisely the difference between benefit to the individual and benefit to the entire church. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 35.1

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: PROPHECY IS MORE USEFUL.
entries.body: Paul could not forbid speaking in tongues, because this is a gift of the Holy Spirit, but the pursuit of prophecy is more acceptable because it is more useful. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: NOT BELITTLING TONGUES.
entries.body: Paul says this in order to show that he is interested in benefiting the church, not in belittling those who have the gift of tongues. He could have this gift himself, but it would be useless and would make no difference to the church if uninterpreted. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 35.2

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: SPEECH REQUIRES PRECISION.
entries.body: If we require precision even in lifeless instruments, how much more will we require it in living beings? {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 35.3

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: MAKE NO UNCERTAIN SOUND.
entries.body: If it is uncertain whether the bugle is sounding for relaxation or for war, nobody will get ready for battle. The latter is how your own words should be understood, for they are meant to prepare soldiers for spiritual warfare. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 14

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: READINESS FOR BATTLE.
entries.body: The trumpet is a sign of war. Therefore, when the soul perceives itself armed with so many and such important virtues, it necessarily goes forth to the war it has against principalities and powers and against the world rulers. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: INTERPRETATION REQUIRED.
entries.body: If speaking in tongues is useless, why was it given? It was given for the benefit of the person who has it. But if it is to help others also, then there must be some interpretation. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 35.4

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: NO LANGUAGE MEANINGLESS.
entries.body: There is no language without meaning because all languages are human. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: MEANING OF A LANGUAGE.
entries.body: Any language we cannot understand we look down on as barbaric. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 14

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: EAGER FOR THE SPIRIT.
entries.body: The soul is stirred and rejoices when it learns something more about the Scriptures. The more it tends in this direction, the more it abandons vices. It is for these reasons that Paul advises that one should make efforts to communicate clearly. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: EDIFYING THE CHURCH.
entries.body: The building up of the church is Paul’s touchstone in everything he says. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 35.5

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: PRAY FOR THE POWER TO INTERPRET.
entries.body: If the one who speaks in tongues does not have the power to interpret them, others will not understand, but he will know what he was moved by the Spirit to say. When this is understood by others as well, there will be fruit from it. Here as elsewhere, we are taught to seek the common good of the church. {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: THE STERILITY OF YESTERDAY’S FLESH.
entries.body: If you have brought a word in praise of God, not new and fresh from the learning of the spirit, from the teaching of God’s grace, your mouth indeed offers a sacrifice of praise, but your mind is accused on account of the sterility of yesterday’s flesh. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE MIND UNFRUITFUL.
entries.body: What can a person achieve if he does not know what he is saying? {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: MY SPIRIT PRAYS.
entries.body: For he speaks thus, when that which is said is not understood, because it cannot even be uttered, unless the images of corporeal sounds precede the oral sounds by the thought of the spirit. {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: PRAY WITH THE SPIRIT AND THE MIND.
entries.body: For our mind would not even be able to pray unless the Spirit prayed for it as if obeying it, so that we cannot even sing and hymn the Father in Christ with proper rhythm, melody, measure and harmony unless the Spirit who searches everything, even the depths of God, first praises and hymns him whose depths he has searched out and has understood as far as he is able. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Gregory of Nazianzus
entries.title: PRAY WITH THE SPIRIT.
entries.body: Worshiping and praying in the Spirit seem to me to be simply the Spirit presenting prayer and worship to himself. {fn:0}

entries.author: Abraham of Nathpar
entries.title: PRAYER BEYOND PSALMODY.
entries.body: It is in spirit and mind that one should pray and sing to God. Paul does not say anything about the tongue. The reason is that this spiritual prayer is not offered up or prayed by the tongue, for it is deeper than the lips and the tongue, more interiorized than any composite sounds, lying beyond psalmody and wisdom. {fn:0}

entries.author: Cassiodorus
entries.title: SING WITH THE SPIRIT.
entries.body: The prayer of those who sing is acceptable to God only if a pure heart carries the same message which the words of the hymn unfold. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: CONFIRMING THE PRAYER.
entries.body: The confirmation of the prayer comes about when people say Amen to it. The words spoken are confirmed in the minds of the hearers by the confession of truth. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: THE WHOLE PEOPLE OF GOD.
entries.body: Paul is thinking here of laypeople, who do not hold office in the church. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 14

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: LANGUAGE SHOULD BLESS EVERYONE.
entries.body: Here Paul explains what he said above, which is that a believer should be a blessing to everyone. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 14

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: PAUL CLAIMS THE GIFT.
entries.body: Paul claims the gift for himself in order to show how unimportant it is. He does the same thing elsewhere when he points out that he is a better-qualified Jew than his critics are.{fn:0} {fn:1}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 35.7

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: SPEAK WITH A CLEAR MIND.
entries.body: Paul wants to speak with a clear mind and in a normal language. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: MATURE INTELLECTUALLY.
entries.body: Paul wants them to be mature intellectually so they will grasp accurately what is needed for the upbuilding of the church. In this way they will leave behind malice and errors, striving instead for the things which are conducive to the good of the brotherhood. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: BE BABES IN EVIL.
entries.body: To be a babe in evil is not even to know what evil is. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 36.1

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: AQUILA AS PAUL’S SOURCE.
entries.body: Note that Paul refers to the prophetic writings also as the law. In this passage he is quoting Isaiah [28:11] according to Aquila’s translation, and not according to the Septuagint. {fn:0}

footnotes.text: JTS 10:38. Paul was as familiar with rabbinic text as with the LXX.

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: FOREKNOWLEDGE OF UNBELIEF.
entries.body: The Lord said this about those whom he knew in advance would not believe in the Savior. For to speak in other tongues and with other lips is to preach the New Testament. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE VEIL OF TONGUES.
entries.body: The utterances of God are hidden beneath the veil of an unknown tongue, so that they should not be seen by unbelievers. Paul was saying that tongues are useful for hiding ideas from unbelievers. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: TONGUES NOT FOR INSTRUCTION.
entries.body: Tongues are a sign to unbelievers not for their instruction, as prophecy is for both believers and unbelievers, but to astonish them. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 36.2

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: TONGUES, PROPHECY AND MIRACLE.
entries.body: Tongues are a miracle in themselves. Prophecy, however, is a miracle in the substance of what it contains but not in the way in which it is uttered. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: St. Basil the Great
entries.title: GOOD ORDER VALUED.
entries.body: Even if a stranger should address his inquiries through ignorance to some other person and although he who is questioned by mistake is able to make a satisfactory reply, yet, for the sake of good order, he should keep silence and direct the stranger to him whose function it is, as the apostles did when the Lord was present. In this way, speech will be employed in a well-ordered and fitting manner. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: TONGUES IN THE ASSEMBLY.
entries.body: On the day of Pentecost the Jews said that the apostles were full of new wine.{fn:0} It is more or less the same thing here. {fn:1}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 14

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: THE UNBELIEVER CONVICTED.
entries.body: The unbeliever is convicted when his conscience is struck by the teaching being given. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 14

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE UNBELIEVER WILL WORSHIP GOD.
entries.body: When he sees that God is being praised and that Christ is being adored and that nothing is disguised or being done in secret, as happens among pagans, he will understand clearly that this is true religion. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: THE SPIRIT IS GOD.
entries.body: Note that here the Holy Spirit is directly called God. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 261

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: FOR EDIFICATION.
entries.body: Particular effort should be made to ensure that unlearned people will benefit. Nothing should be done to hide things from them because of their lack of learning. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: TIME FOR SCRIPTURE INTERPRETATION.
entries.body: Paul does not want these people to take up the whole day and leave insufficient time for expounding the Scriptures. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: IF ANY SPEAK IN A TONGUE.
entries.body: Paul does not forbid speaking in tongues, however much he may belittle the gift, but he insists that it be kept under control and used for the edification of the whole church. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 36.5

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: IF THERE IS NO INTERPRETER.
entries.body: The person who speaks in the Holy Spirit speaks when he chooses to do so and then can be silent, like the prophets. But those who are possessed by an unclean spirit speak even when they do not want to. They say things that they do not understand. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: St. Cyprian of Carthage
entries.title: RESTRAINING OBSTINATE STRIFE.
entries.body: Each one ought not to strive obstinately for that which he learned and once held, but if anything better or more useful should exist, he should embrace it willingly. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: ASKING QUESTIONS.
entries.body: Paul allowed others to ask questions about ambiguous matters, so that they might be elucidated by clearer discussion. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: ALLOW A FAIR HEARING.
entries.body: In particular, the higher-ranking person should give way to the lower. It is simply not the case that every privilege can be granted to a single individual. Nor can it be that anyone, however low in the hierarchy, should have nothing given to him. No one is without the grace of God. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: PROPHESY ONE BY ONE.
entries.body: It is a tradition of the synagogue which Paul is asking us to follow, whereby the people dispute while seated in chairs, on benches or on the floor, according to their rank. If a revelation has been given to someone sitting on the floor, he should be allowed to speak and not be despised because of his low rank. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: TAKE TURNS.
entries.body: This rule is still applied in the church today, in that preachers take turns to teach the people. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 262

entries.author: Cassiodorus
entries.title: THAT ALL MAY LEARN.
entries.body: Clearly the prophet builds up the church when through the function of his foretelling he makes wholly clear matters exceedingly vital which were unknown. Those who have been granted the ability to understand well and to interpret the divine Scriptures are obviously not excluded from the gift of prophecy. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE SPIRITS OF PROPHETS.
entries.body: The Spirit is said to be subject so that he may facilitate the good efforts which he prompts. {fn:0}

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: THE GIFT OF PROPHECY.
entries.body: If the gift is subject to the prophets, how can it not also be subject to you, so that you may keep quiet when you are meant to? {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.body: As for the spirits of the prophets, these are given to them by God and are spoken of as being in a way their property, as “The spirits of the prophets are subject to the prophets” [1 Cor 14:32] and “The spirit of Elijah rested upon Elisha” [2 Kgs 2:15]. Thus, it is said, there is nothing absurd in supposing that John, “in the spirit and power of Elijah,” turned the hearts of the fathers to the children and that it was on account of this spirit that he was called “Elijah who is to come”.

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: AIM FOR PATIENCE.
entries.body: Those who are called in peace ought to aim for patience, so that the laws of peace are not broken. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: EMPTY BOXES THAT ONCE STORED WEALTH.
entries.body: Truly, the church was more like heaven in Paul’s time, because the Spirit governed everything and moved each one of the members in turn. But now it seems we have only the symbols of those gifts. We also have only two or three speaking in the service, but these are only a pale shadow of what prevailed then. The present church is like a woman who has fallen from her former, prosperous days and who retains only the outward signs of that prosperity, displaying the boxes and caskets in which she kept her wealth, but which are now empty. This is true not only in the matter of gifts but in life and virtue as well. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 36.7

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: HOW ARE WOMEN TO KEEP SILENCE?
entries.body: If this was the case, what are we to make of the fact that Philip had four daughters who prophesied?{fn:0} If they could do it, why can we not let our own prophetesses speak? We may answer this question as follows. First, if our prophetesses have spoken, show us the signs of prophecy in them. Second, even if the daughters of Philip did prophesy, they did not do so inside the church. Likewise in the Old Testament, although Deborah was reputed to be a prophetess,{fn:1} there is no indication that she ever corporately addressed the people in the way that Isaiah or Jeremiah did. The same is true of Huldah.{fn:2} {fn:3}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: DESIRE TOWARD HER HUSBAND.
entries.body: Where does the law say this? In Genesis [3:16]: Your desire shall be toward your husband, and he shall rule over you. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 37.1

entries.author: St. Ephrem the Syrian
entries.title: EVE’S MOUTH WILL YET SERVE.
entries.body: The serpent is crippled and bound by the curse, while Eve’s mouth is sealed with a silence that is beneficial, but she will also serve again as a harp to sing the praises of the Creator. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: WOMEN VEILED IN CHURCH TO APPEAR HUMBLE.
entries.body: It is shameful because it is contrary to discipline for them to presume to speak about the law in the house of God, who has taught that they are subject to their husbands, when they know that men have the primacy there and that for them it is more fitting to be free to pray while holding their tongues. If they dare to speak in church, it is a disgrace, because they are veiled in order to appear humble. Moreover, women like this show that they are immodest, which is a disgrace to their husbands too. For when women are insolent, their husbands receive the blame as well. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: WOMEN ORDERLY, HUSBANDS ATTENTIVE.
entries.body: A woman is softer of mind than a man and more subject to being flooded with emotion. Thus he sets husbands as protectors for the benefit of both. Paul calls for husbands to be attentive and for women to be orderly. In this way husbands would have to communicate with their wives about exactly what they heard. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 37.1

entries.author: St. Cyril of Jerusalem
entries.title: LET PARADISE OPEN.
entries.body: I wish to see each man’s earnestness and each woman’s devotion. Burn out impiety from your mind, put your soul on the anvil and your stubborn infidelity under the hammer. . . . Then let the gate of paradise be opened to each man and each woman among you. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: FILLED WITH VANITY.
entries.body: Paul issued the Corinthians with this reproof because they were so elated with vanity. The suggestion was that if they did not obey the words of the faith, there would be no one who would believe. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: ARE YOU THE ONLY ONES?
entries.body: Here Paul introduces the example of the other churches, indicating that Corinth has no right to claim exceptional status. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 37.2

entries.author: Montanist Oracles
entries.title: PROPHETESSES RESTRAINED IN SPEAKING TO THE PEOPLE.
entries.body: It is attested that Deborah was a prophetess. And Mariam, the sister of Aaron, took the timbrel and led the women. But you would not find that Deborah addressed the people as Jeremiah and Isaiah did. You would not find that Huldah spoke to the people although she was a prophetess. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Catenae on Paul’s Epistles to the Corinthians 14.36

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: A COMMAND OF THE LORD.
entries.body: Paul is alluding to the false apostles mentioned above, by whom they had been misled. They were teaching things which people wanted to hear but which were not of God. By saying that he was handing on nothing of his own, Paul was trying to indicate that what he was saying came from God and not from men. He therefore preaches consistently, with a clear conscience because he does not desire to please men but God. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: RECOGNIZE FOR WHOM THE APOSTLE SPEAKS.
entries.body: Anyone who does not recognize that what the apostle says is from God will not be recognized on the day of judgment. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: EARNESTLY DESIRE PROPHECY.
entries.body: In order to console them after all his rebukes, Paul calls them brothers and encourages them to have a desire to prophesy, so that by frequent discussion and exposition of the divine law they may become better equipped to be able to learn that what the false apostles were teaching was perverse. {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: NOTHING DISORDERED.
entries.body: Therefore he wants all these to be set in order by you, nothing disordered, nothing restless, nothing indecent. . . . Therefore, now our true high priest, Christ, also wants his hands to be filled with finely composed incense.{fn:0} {fn:1}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: WHAT IS DECENT?
entries.body: Something is done decently when it is done peacefully and with discipline. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Basil the Great
entries.title: WELL-ORDERED WAY OF LIFE.
entries.body: Paul is referring to the decent and well-ordered way of life in the society of the faithful, where the relationship which obtains among the members of the body is maintained. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: GOOD ORDER BUILDS UP.
entries.body: Nothing builds up as much as good order, peace and love, just as nothing is more destructive than their opposites. It is not only in spiritual affairs but in everything that one may observe this. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 37.4

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: RESISTING DISSENSION.
entries.body: Those who make dissensions and disturbances in the church are the ones who seem to be what they are not. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: THE FELLOWSHIP OF THE LAWGIVER.
entries.body: Law and order come first. Let us also dedicate ourselves to this principle as members of the fellowship of the great Lawgiver. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 264

entries.author: St. Fulgentius of Ruspe
entries.title: DISORDERING GOD’S ORDER.
entries.body: If we take a closer look at the origin of sin, I think that it is nothing else than the inordinate love by a rational creature of the things set in order by God. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Irenaeus of Lyons
entries.body: [The heretic Marcus], handing mixed cups to the women, bids them to consecrate these in his presence. When this has been done, he produces another cup of much larger size than the one the deluded woman has consecrated, and pouring from the smaller one consecrated by the woman into the one brought forward by himself, he at the same time pronounces these words, “May that Charis who is before all things, and who transcends all knowledge and speech, fill your inner man, and multiply in you her own knowledge, by sowing the grain of mustard seed in you as in good soil.” Repeating certain other similar words, and thus goading on the wretched woman [to madness], he then appears a worker of wonders when the large cup is seen to have been filled out of the small one, so as even to overflow by what has been obtained from it. By accomplishing several other similar things, he has completely deceived many, and drawn them away after him.

entries.author: Tertullian
entries.body: [I]t matters not to them, however different their treatment of subjects, provided that they can conspire together to storm the citadel of the one truth. All are puffed up, all offer you knowledge. Their catechumens are perfect before they are fully taught. The very women of these heretics, how wanton they are! For they are bold enough to teach, to dispute, to enact exorcisms, to undertake cures.

entries.author: Tertullian
entries.body: [A female heretic], lately conversant in this quarter, has carried away a great number with her most venomous doctrine, making it her aim to destroy baptism. This is in accordance with nature; for vipers and asps . . . themselves generally live in arid and waterless places. But we, little fishes after the example of our [great] fish, Jesus Christ, are born in water, and find safety in no other way than by permanently abiding in water; so that most monstrous creature, who had no right to teach even sound doctrine, knew full well how to kill the little fishes, by taking them away from the water!

entries.author: Tertullian
entries.body: It is not permitted for a woman to speak in the church [1 Cor 14:34–35], but neither [is it permitted her] . . . to offer, nor to claim to herself a lot in any manly function, not to say sacerdotal office.

entries.author: Didascalia
entries.body: It is neither right nor necessary therefore that women should be teachers, and especially about the name of Christ and the redemption of his Passion. For you have not been appointed to this, O women, and especially widows, that you should teach, but that you should pray and entreat the Lord God. For he the Lord God, Jesus Christ our teacher, sent us the Twelve to instruct the people and the Gentiles; and there were with us women disciples, Mary Magdalene and Mary the daughter of James, and the other Mary; but he did not send them to instruct the people with us.

entries.author: Firmilian of Caesarea
entries.body: [T]here suddenly arose among us a certain woman, who in a state of ecstasy announced herself as a prophetess and acted as if filled with the Holy Spirit. . . . Through the deceptions and illusions of the demon, this woman had previously set about deluding believers in a variety of ways. Among the means by which she had deluded many was daring to pretend that, through proper invocation, she consecrated bread and performed the Eucharist. She offered up the sacrifice to the Lord in a liturgical act that corresponds to the usual rites, and she baptized many, all the while misusing the customary and legitimate wording of the [baptismal] question. She carried all these things out in such a manner that nothing seemed to deviate from the norms of the Church.
